reviews.review_text: My friend bought the game for me. It's the perfect game for multiple players and it's a lot of fun. Can't believe I have played it for more than 1000 hours , still feel new to me.

This game has several modes that can be accessed to make the gameplay either easier or more challenging. I once played it with three of my friends, and it was very difficult when we first tried it because of the game mechanics and each character’s different skills. Every player has their own role, which makes the game very fun to play as a co-op game. Highly recommended for co-op gameplay.
its a great game and its even better with friends, soo... anyone wants to play with me (EU servers)

The original Don't Starve was a fairly tight survival experience and a joy to explore. It's Rogue inspiration could at time makes the permadeath demoralising, though its more contained nature made it fairly easy to drop back in for another go, and the victory conditions present made the game feel cohesive; it had a start, middle and end.

Unfortunately, the multiplayer version of this game has become monstrously complicated and fairly bloated as a result of constant updates. Character revamps and system revamps are frequently implemented, often revamping characters or systems which had already previously been revamped. Revamp! Certainly the game is well cared for and you can most likely look forward to years more updates, though you'll probably need a wiki to even begin to understand what to do.
idk bro, definelty not good to go in blind, but a very good and fun gameplay loop
You'll love this brain teaser game! I thought it's childish until you're fully immerse, sadly it doesn't have achievements but hey, focus on playing! 😁

best farming system in any survival game ever made

so good i die to the simplest of other things, cause im spending 99% of the time speaking to potatoes instead of fighting / getting resources / preparing for winter or bosses

wouldnt change a thing

At first, it looks like just another survival game. But the deeper you dive, the more you realize how much content and hidden lore it actually has.

The world seems mysterious, with pieces of history scattered everywhere, waiting for you to put them together yourself. It's not just about survival – it's about finding out what this world is really about.

There's always something new to explore, new mechanics to master, and new secrets to uncover. That's what makes this game so captivating for me.
Its Just amazing, The gameplay is great and the survivalness part of it is way to good

mega fun to play with friends struggling to survive while dealing with a billion diffrent problems
not to mention the characters and artstyle is SUPER charming and cute! i love it alot! 

plus the devs are actually amazing and give you a second copy you can gift a friend for FREE when you buy the game!!!  i cant wait for dont starve elsewhere :D
I played for 5-6 years, the clan broke, access to the servers. I fixed the servers, but I don't have access to the characters. The game itself is even very good.

Graphics 10 out of 10!

good game, fun with friends been on my radar for so long
if you are into games like this, pick it up you will not regret!
Its fun. Confusing yes, but still fun. Grab a few other friends and go play it.
it's fun but my friend refuses to play it with me so i cant really like say anything about it

I enjoy how this game manages to have such a different feel from the singleplayer version of the game. There is so much more content here, and it's so fun to play with friends. This is a pretty difficult game to get into initially though, since it is so hard.

Happy 10th Anniversary, Don't Starve Together.

I've been playing since 2016, and it's hard to believe ten years have gone by. This game gave me countless great memories, unforgettable adventures, and most importantly, some amazing friends.

I couldn't post this on the anniversary because of internet restrictions in my country, but even 40 days later, I still wanted to say thank you.

Thank you, Klei. See you in the Constant.

Takes some time learning progression and has a kind of gameplay loop that takes a "dying is part of the learning process" mindset. But after the hardships it is by far, one of the most addicting games i've played in a long time, friends make this game SIGNIFICANTLY more fun but isn't required to enjoy. If your looking for a time sink i would highly recommend this game ESPECIALLY if you have friends, 10/10 would eat wormwood again.
This game was awesome, can play with friends and family , its tough game , we have to thinks ahead of time to survive this game, explore all the part of the world. Its challenging.
Really really good fun. But the game doesnt teach you much. You either have to work it out all on your own through a lot of deaths, or watch guides that teach you strats.
Very good and challenging game. Finally beat night 1.

I’ve been playing this game for the past four years, and I still love it to this day! I truly enjoy its mechanics and how replayable it is, thanks to both its world-building and character systems. I’ve recommended this game to several friends, and they ended up loving it as well. Without a doubt, I highly recommend it and will continue recommending it!

+ A good game

- Very complicated, best way to start out is watching those 1 hour intro videos, yes 1 hour intro to start out since you have to know 100 things & be prepared for the deadly deadlines or die and restart. Like on day 30 winter boss will spawn and just destroy your base 1 shotting all structures. Also the weather, winter itself is deadly.

Ofc if you already played it a lot and know everything then it is easy, but you need to learn 100 things first and keep up with the weather & bosses.
Always loved this game, and the original. All I would want is more insight points before maxing out.

A pretty tedious game with high skill floor to start with if you want to do well in.

Oh, and unlike the original game it has bosses and creatures with double, triple, or even ten times more health due to "multiplayer balance" which makes playing the game on your own without balance mods to have a considerable difficulty gap (mainly in preparing resources for them) over it.

Anyway that aside, it's basically like playing Terraria but with mediumcore (the difficulty where you drop your inventory when you die) by default, and with much slower progression with more emphasis on survival.

Bit of a banger, like terraria except survival is (very) hard, especially in the beginning, but the movement and boss-fight mechanics are a lot simpler. The fighting mechanics are even a bit boring at times, but lots of complex survival mechanics makes up for it imo.

Tried it for the first time when a friend recommended we explore other multiplayer games besides our usual. Small learning curve in the beginning but for the most part everything is very intuitive and easy once you get the hang of it. There are a lot of straightforward guides online so you're never lost on how to do  the more complicated things and once summarized tough things are actually very simple to understand after a 2 minute google search. The game is overall fun and have recommended to other friends

I first played this game on console and have most of my hours there, but this game has been there for me since 2019 and it is hands down one of the, no, THE greatest survival game I have ever played in my life. I have 2000+ hours and I loved every second of it. Making new friends and meeting others, messing around, felling major bosses with friends, randoms, or solo always felt great. First playing this game and being as experienced as i am now are vastly different experiences but I still manage to enjoy this game and its updates its gotten. I highly recommend this game for those who love overcoming challenges, for its replayability, and for its community.

[h1]This Is Not a Game, It Is a Relentless Stress Simulator[/h1]

[b]The Verdict:[/b] I bought this game thinking it was a cute, Tim Burton-esque cartoon simulator about camping with friends. I was wrong. It is a psychological horror engine designed to test the limits of human friendship and destroy your sanity. 10/10.

[hr][/hr]

[h2]The Reality: Accurate Title Is Accurate[/h2]

[b]You Will Starve. Together.[/b] The name of the game is a literal promise, not a suggestion. You spawn into a beautiful forest, pick up three twigs, think [i]"Wow, this is cozy,"[/i] and then five minutes later the sun goes down, Charlie tears your face off in the pitch black, and a Pig Man chases you down like a debt collector.

[b]Your Friends Are an Active Threat:[/b] You think playing with a squad will make survival easier? Absolutely not. Your friends are not help; they are ravenous, walking resource-vacuums. You will spend three days meticulously gathering berries and roasting meatballs, only for your buddy to eat the entire icebox in ten seconds, accidentally light the base on fire because they were playing with a torch, and then die to a frog.

[b]Weather-Induced Depression:[/b] Every single season is just a different color of pure, unadulterated suffering. Winter freezes your ass off, Summer literally sets your house on fire out of spite, and Spring rains on you until your character gets wet, loses their mind, and starts hallucinating shadow monsters that slap them to death.

[hr][/hr]

[h2]The Mechanics: A Wiki Simulator[/h2]

[b]Don't Fight the Wildlife:[/b] You see a tiny spider and think, [i]"I can take him."[/i] You punch it once. The spider immediately calls 400 of his cousins out of a giant web fortress, and they beat you into a pathetic sausage. You run away, crawl into a swamp for safety, and get instantly violated by a giant subterranean tentacle.

[b]Maxwell Is Laughing At You:[/b] If you manage to survive all of that, the game decides you are having too much fun and sends a giant one-eyed deer-monster (Deerclops) to stomp through your campsite at 3:00 AM, crushing your chests, shattering your science machines, and leaving you crying in the snow.

[hr][/hr]

[b]The Bottom Line:[/b]
If you want to test if your relationship can survive a localized famine, or if you want to stay up until 4:00 AM screaming [i]"WHO ATE THE LAST FLINT?!"[/i] into Discord, this is the greatest multiplayer game ever made. It is brutal, unforgiving, completely chaotic, and deeply addictive. Buy it, gift a copy to a friend you secretly hate, and prepare to perish over an imaginary carrot.

In the distant 2016, it was interesting to play this game, but now the realities are different and there is a feeling that the developers abandoned the game in the same 2016...
The interface suffers greatly, you don't understand what to choose and how to start the game right away...
But the dumbest thing is - I died, became a ghost and had to fly all over the huge map for about half an hour in search of a totem stone to be able to resurrect (while my comrades were looking for supplies), and in the end I didn't find it (maybe I would have flown further and more than an hour would have been spent on all this)

In general, 1/10

for a game thats meant to be played TOGETHER, it does a horrible job at it. my 300 hours as of writing this review are in solo mode, because the way the game was made somehow makes multiplayer really bad. its got EXTREME delay when playing "together." ive tried joining countless servers, but the delay always remains. if you attack an npc, expect to be killed in the time it takes to run away, due to the delay. i pray to god they fix this delay in dont starve elsewhere, if they dont, im not spending a penny on it.
Great Game! There's so much to be done. I've played for so long and it still feels like I'm just scratching the surface

I was expecting a Don't Starve experience with a multiplayer twist, which I got to a certain degree, but not quite.

Maybe if I had started playing when it was new and experienced all the new features as they were released, I wouldn't be so overwhelmed by the ungodly amount of crafting, various machines, and everything else. One huge problem I have is that there is no endgame defense for your precious base. I know that the original Don't Starve pushed you to be rather mobile, but after some time, you could make your home almost untouchable.

In Don't Starve Together, that is simply not the case. Although you can discover an endless number of features, there are no defense towers. Even when you manage to protect your home, the game constantly introduces new ways to destroy hours of effort.

Yes, you can object that survival and roguelike elements are the core of this game, but there is no joy when you have to constantly navigate the game wiki and pray that it won't throw another godlike enemy at you who will trash all your chests.

I don't mind playing hard games, but I figured out that I hate playing games that won't let you keep what you created - at least to a certain degree, and that is exactly what DST does.

That said, even when I played it with friends, we got annoyed after a while and moved on.

The most charming, hand-drawn nightmare that will make you wonder if your friends are actually trying to sabotage you.

Don't Starve Together is a masterpiece of atmospheric survival that feels like a playable Tim Burton sketchbook. The dark, scratchy art style and the eerie, whimsical soundtrack create a world that is as beautiful as it is hostile. Unlike other survival games that give you a gentle pat on the back, DST unceremoniously drops you into the "Constant" and waits for you to make a mistake. Whether you are playing as the well-rounded Wilson, the fire-starting Willow, or the massive, spider-whispering Webber, every character brings a unique flavor to the struggle. It captures a specific kind of dread where the setting sun isn't just a lighting change—it’s a countdown to the moment Charlie comes to find you in the dark.  

The true genius of the game lies in its unforgiving complexity and the "Meatball Economy." There is a steep, jagged learning curve that requires you to master everything from seasonal preparation to the intricacies of the crock pot. You’ll spend your first few hours dying to hounds, freezing in the winter, or accidentally burning your entire base down because someone left a campfire too close to a chest. But once you start coordinating—assigning one person to be the "base mom" who cooks and organizes, while others go out to hunt bosses or explore the caves—it becomes one of the most rewarding co-op experiences ever made. It’s a game that turns "not dying" into a complex logistical triumph.  

In 2026, Klei Entertainment’s commitment to this game is nothing short of legendary. Even years after its release, the game is still receiving massive updates, character reworks, and seasonal events that keep the world feeling fresh. While some veteran players argue that the "lore-heavy" updates and newer mechanics like the Lunar Island have shifted the game away from its simple survival roots, there is no denying the sheer volume of content available. Whether you’re sailing the high seas to fight the Crab King or just trying to survive your first Autumn without going insane, Don't Starve Together remains the gold standard for the genre. Just remember: if you see a giant deer with one eye and massive antlers, don't try to pet it.
